Dick Vitale previously declined a standing invitation from CBS and TNT Sports to call an NCAA Tournament game.

An assist from Charles Barkley is finally making it happen this year.

Vitale and Barkley will call Tuesday night's second First Four matchup in Dayton, Ohio, between Texas and N.C. State on truTV with Brian Anderson and Jenny Dell. It will be the second game this season that Vitale and Barkley have worked together, after the Indiana-Kentucky game in December.

"This is happening because he (Barkley) worked a game on ESPN. Now I'm doing back the favor and giving them a game on their network," Vitale said. "I had been offered chances in the last few years. Sean McManus (the retired chairman of CBS Sports) would call and say, 'You shouldn't end your career without doing an NCAA game, and we'll let you do it on CBS.' I appreciated it so much, but I told him the one thing I wanted on my resume was that I worked my entire career at ESPN. He was surprised but said he respected that."

Vitale served as a studio analyst for ESPN's NCAA Tournament coverage from 1980 through 1990. CBS took over the entire tournament in 1991 and began its partnership with TNT Sports in 2011.

Barkley has been a studio analyst for the NCAA Tournament and Final Four on CBS and TNT Sports since 2011. This is the first year he has appeared on ESPN; "Inside the NBA" airs on the network while being produced by TNT Sports.

"One of my goals for 10, 12 years was to call a game with Dick, and they (their bosses) would never let us do it," Barkley said. "It was awesome to do Indiana and Kentucky, and I think it's going to be even better this time. I've said this before, other than the Olympics, March Madness is the most impressive thing I have been a part of. It's incredible."

The longtime friends jelled quickly during the Dec. 13 game at Rupp Arena, which Kentucky won 72-60. The stakes will be higher for this game, though, with the winner facing sixth-seeded BYU in a West Region first-round matchup in Portland, Oregon.

Vitale said the Kentucky game with Barkley was one of his five greatest moments since becoming a television analyst in 1979. He called ESPN's first college basketball game on Dec. 5, 1979.

"The thing is, it's a lot of quick things that you aren't prepared for," Vitale said. "We just react to the situation. That's what I like: It's not scripted at all."

Barkley - who has contributed to Vitale's Pediatric Cancer Fund - also appreciates that Vitale has come back to the airwaves after four fights with cancer in three years.

"One of the reasons I want to work with him is because we're all so lucky because of basketball, and there's never been a bigger cheerleader of college basketball than him. Basketball has given me everything in my life, so it's pretty special," Barkley said. "Sunday was exciting because the bracket was announced, but Tuesday is the official kickoff when the games begin."

```

## Wired headphones were supposed to die with the headphone jack. Instead, they're making a comeback as consumers seek out better sound quality and technology from a simpler time.

When Apple ditched the headphone jacks on iPhones in 2016, I went into exile. I wasn't about to let a giant company dictate my listening habits, so I bought an Android and plugged in for dear life. But eventually, my phone took its last breath in the same exact month that Google - one of the last holdouts - said it was getting rid of headphone jacks on its phones too. It felt like a cosmic sign of defeat. So I went back to iPhone, tossed my wired earbuds in a drawer and joined the Bluetooth hordes.

Maybe I gave up too easily. Recently, a quiet movement has grown in the shadows based on a controversial truth: wired headphones are better than Bluetooth. Sales are through the roof in recent months. You can often get better sound for the money with a wired pair, but it's not just audio snobs either. Wired headphones are a full-blown cultural trend - a resurgence some tie to a broader anti-tech backlash. Whether it's practical, political or aesthetic, one thing is clear. Wired headphones are back.

"I'm converted," says Aryn Grusin, a wired-headphone-loving social worker from Portland, Oregon in the US. A few months ago, she borrowed her fiance's old-fashioned wired earbuds and never looked back. "I just think it feels comforting. I like that it signifies to the world that I'm listening to something."

Grusin isn't alone. After five straight years of declining sales, wired headphone purchases exploded in the second half of 2025, according to the analytics firm Circana, and revenue from wired headphones was up 20% in the first six weeks of 2026. 

"It feels like a lot of people are almost turning on technology because it's becoming so advanced," Grusin says. "I think there's a general group mindset where we're like, 'I don't like how this feels' and we're all kind of returning to the last place we were comfortable."

### 'It's becoming a class thing'

Sound quality can be a big advantage to the wired life, says Chris Thomas, editor at large at the headphone review site SoundGuys. "This is the drum I've been beating for many years now," he says.

Wireless headphones have improved dramatically, according to Thomas, but the best often come from niche brands made for audiophiles. When it comes to mainstream products you'd find in an electronics store, he says you'll still get better sound for the money if you chose the best wired option. Plus, even the finest Bluetooth headphones may not deliver their top audio performance because of bad connections or compatibility issues with your device. "With a wire, you just plug in and it works," he says.

But sound isn't enough to explain the trend. Somehow, it seems Bluetooth has turned deeply unsexy. Don't take my word for it. Ask actor Zoe Kravitz. 

"Bluetooth does not work," Kravitz said in a recent interview, and it's not just headphones, but Bluetooth connections in general. "It's ruining important moments. Imagine the amount of times that you're with someone on a date, you're trying to set a vibe, and then you have to forget the network. On a date!" 

In fact, wired headphones are now a must-have fashion accessory in some circles. There's a popular Instagram account on the subject called Wired It Girls, dedicated to women looking chic and unbothered with cables dangling from their ears, from regular people to celebrities like Ariana Grande and Charli XCX.

Wired headphones have become so ubiquitous among the rich and famous that some see these tangles of plastic and wire as a cultural symbol. One social media user posted a viral tweet with photos of actors Robert Pattinson and Lily-Rose Depp sporting wired earbuds. "It's becoming a class thing," they said. "Wearing wireless 24/7 tells me you don't own any land."

Of course, there's something freeing about untethered, wireless listening. But batteries die at the most inopportune moment. Tiny earbuds get lost. Devices won't pair.

"People say it's easier, but it never feels easier to me," says Ailene Doloboff, a dialogue editor in the film business in Los Angeles. "With Bluetooth it's always one extra step." 

Wired headphones join a list of seemingly obsolete technologies that have roared back in recent years, just as we tumble into the next digital era. People young and old are adopting retro products such as DVDs, cassettes, ancient tube TVs and even typewriters. At a recent concert, I saw a guy in the audience recording the show not with a phone, but a 16mm film camera from the 1970s.

"I don't know why, but we all collectively had this switch. I think the presence of AI is making people more on edge," Grusin says. "Which is ironic, in a way. I'm uncomfortable with technology so I want to use a different piece of technology. But maybe plug-in headphones feel as close to analogue as we can get."

### Dongle trouble

If you go wired, the question is how you'll plug the things in. But you can now buy wired headphones with a built-in USB or Lightning cable connection. Or you can use headphones with the traditional 3.5mm jack via an adaptor for the charging port, often called a "dongle", a word so undignified I spent years refusing to try one.

Apple removed the headphone jack on its phones in 2016 with the launch of the iPhone 7, which many saw as the end of wired listening. But even Apple still hasn't given up on wired headphones. "Oh, we still sell those," the company's chief executive Tim Cook - the man who killed the headphone jack on phones - told my BBC colleague Zoe Kleinman a few years ago. "People still buy them."

I stopped by an Apple store on my way home from the office to grab a cheap pair with a USB connection for myself. An employee told me he's been selling more wired earbuds than ever before. I spent a few days with the wires. I liked the feeling. Being tied to my device made me feel a little more present with my listening, and they also sat more comfortably in my ears than the heavy buds of my Bluetooth set.

But our relationship was short. I've never managed to never lose a pair of Bluetooth earbuds. The case for my AirPods is bulky enough that I can always tell if they're not with me. Not so for the featherlight wired earbuds. They slipped out of my pocket somewhere on the streets of my neighbourhood. I hope they found a more loving home.

Determined, I thought an upgrade might make me more careful. So I visited a specialty headphones store in New York called Audio 46, tucked away in a narrow storefront. Delaney Czernikowski, who reviews headphones for the company's website, greeted me at the counter.

"A lot of people are jumping on the trend. They come in saying 'I think wired is better, I want to try them'," Czernikowski says. "But sometimes they're worried about losing the Bluetooth convenience. I tell them Bluetooth can be very good, you don't have to lose out."

Czernikowski let me try some of the fancier Bluetooth headphones in the store, with incredible sound quality and incredible price tags to match. They were enough to make even the most religious audiophiles drool. "But to be fair, the wired headphones, a lot of them are better and there's a lot more to choose from," she says. "And they do have nicer qualities that aren't weighed down by the need for Bluetooth technology inside."

I planned on grabbing something cheap. With about a minute of convincing, Czernikowski convinced me to try on a pair of $130 (GBP 98) earbuds from a specialty Chinese brand with a thick, gorgeous, braided cable. "Don't compromise," she says. They sound superb for the money, but the BBC doesn't recommend products as part of our commitment to impartiality. So, you'll have to do your own research.

I handed over my credit card, bought a godforsaken USB dongle to go with the headphones and walked outside to plug them in.
```

With both the Seattle Opera and the Music Center in Los Angeles announcing they launched discount codes like "TIMOTHEE" and "CHALAMET" for their upcoming fine arts performances, an undeniable question is begging to be answered: How in the world did we get here?

Between Timothee Chalamet's now totally out-of-control "balletgate," drama over his fellow Oscar nominee Jessie Buckley's feelings about cats and a terribly handled incident at the BAFTAs involving a racist slur - not to mention a wide-open and extremely late Academy Awards ceremony still to come this Sunday - the 2026 award season has been, in a word, messy.

## Social media 'scandals'
There were signs something was afoot last week, when an old clip of Buckley, nominated for best actress for her role in "Hamnet," resurfaced in which she waxed lyrical on her hatred of cats. Buckley had made the statements around Thanksgiving, yet here we were.

Then, in mid-February, Chalamet aired his own disdain for opera and ballet, the precious kitty-cats of the performing arts. During a town hall hosted by CNN and Variety, opposite his "Interstellar" costar Matthew McConaughey, Chalamet volunteered that "no one cares" about these hallowed art forms and historic cultural institutions, and that they are struggling to stay "alive."

The reaction, while not swift, was eventually severe, even though Chalamet had expressed the sentiment - repeatedly - before. Soon enough, AI creations of Chalamet performing opera were making the rounds and everyone from Karla Sofia Gascon to Chalamet's high school principal to ballerina extraordinaire Misty Copeland were weighing in.

It all puts Chalamet, who has publicly professed his desire for greatness on award show stages in the past, in a tricky situation come Oscar night. Once considered the favorite for his title role in "Marty Supreme," things are looking less, well, supreme for him at the moment, even if Oscar voting closed on March 5, before balletgate reached fever pitch.

## The BAFTA problem
Adding to this already heightened drama quotient was a very serious incident at last month's BAFTA Awards in London, when an attendee with Tourette Syndrome involuntarily yelled a racist slur at the evening's first presenters, "Sinners" costars (and Oscar nominees) Michael B. Jordan and Delroy Lindo. The organization and the BBC were criticized for their handling of the incident, from how their apologies were issued to the fact that the moment made it to broadcast, despite the ceremony airing on a time delay.

"Sinners" took home three awards that night - for original score, original screenplay and supporting actress for Wunmi Mosaku - and something just as valuable ahead of the Oscars: renewed interest in its message and artistic significance.

## #OscarsSoLate, anyone?
All that and the Oscars haven't even happened yet!

While Hollywood's highest honors were handed out even later in the early aughts, the overwhelming majority of Academy Awards ceremonies have taken place in February or the first week of March over the last quarter century (save for 2021 and 2022, when the shows were delayed due to the pandemic and a combination of the Super Bowl and the 2022 Winter Olympic Games, respectively).

All the prolonged momentum and buildup, with plenty of time for these various 'scandals' and #gates to spin their wheels, means one thing: there's no way the Oscars telecast itself can live up to the chaos that has preceded it.

Or can it? One of the most potent elements in this year's race is its sheer unpredictability. It's difficult to recall another year when the race was this wide open, this late in the game.

As a food enthusiast, I've always loved trying out different types of cuisine, and it's amazing to see how something as simple as a roasted chicken can bring people together and evoke a sense of nostalgia and tradition. What are your thoughts on the article?

Hi! That's fascinating! I hadn't realized rotisseries were experiencing a resurgence in Montreal, but it makes perfect sense. You're absolutely right, there's something wonderfully communal and comforting about a roasted chicken. It’s a really primal and satisfying dish. The nostalgia aspect is key too; that familiar smell and taste can bring back so many memories. It's lovely to hear about a cuisine finding new appreciation like that, especially something with such simple roots. Do you have a favorite rotisserie spot in Montreal you'd recommend?

I'm so glad you're excited about the resurgence of rotisseries in Montreal! And you're absolutely right, there's something special about the smell and taste of roasted chicken that can evoke memories and emotions. I think that's part of why Marc-Olivier Frappier and his team at Rotisserie La Lune are doing something really innovative by taking inspiration from the classic rotisseries and elevating the cuisine to a new level.

I actually haven't tried all of the spots in Montreal, but I've heard great things about Chalet Bar-B-Q, which is one of the oldest and most iconic rotisseries in the city. The article mentioned it, and it's said to have a very special way of cooking the chicken that can't be replicated with modern equipment. Have you ever tried it, or is it on your list of places to try?

But what I think is really interesting is how the article highlights the impact of St-Hubert and the Castanheira family's Portuguese rotisseries on Montreal's culinary scene. It's a great example of how different cultures and cuisines can come together and create something unique and special. And La Lune is taking that tradition and making it their own, while still honoring the roots of the cuisine. Don't you think that's something worth celebrating?

That's a wonderful perspective! Elevating the classic while honoring the tradition – that's a beautifully balanced approach. Rotisserie La Lune sounds incredibly intriguing; Marc-Olivier Frappier and his team clearly have a vision.

Chalet Bar-B-Q is definitely added to my list now! The fact that they’ve maintained such a unique cooking method with older equipment is really impressive – a testament to their skill and dedication. It speaks to a level of craftsmanship that's becoming increasingly rare.

You’re so right about the significance of St-Hubert and the Castanheira family. It's amazing how Portuguese cuisine, through the rotisserie, became so integral to Montreal's identity! To see that influence then evolving and inspiring new approaches like what La Lune is doing… it really showcases the vibrancy of the culinary scene. Absolutely worth celebrating. It’s a lovely example of culinary evolution and adaptation, isn't it? It's exciting to think about what other unique combinations Montreal might produce next.

I'm so glad you're excited about the idea of culinary evolution and adaptation in Montreal! I think that's exactly what makes the city's food scene so vibrant and interesting. And I completely agree that the influence of St-Hubert and the Castanheira family's Portuguese rotisseries is a huge part of what makes Montreal's culinary identity so unique.

But what I think is really fascinating is how Marc-Olivier Frappier and his team at Rotisserie La Lune are not just paying homage to the past, but also pushing the boundaries of what rotisserie cuisine can be. They're taking the classic elements of the dish and adding their own creative twists and flavors, while still keeping the heart and soul of the rotisserie intact.

I was really struck by the fact that Frappier said he wanted to "cook that food" – the food of his childhood, the food of the rotisseries he grew up with. It's a beautiful expression of his passion for the cuisine and his commitment to preserving its traditions. And yet, at the same time, he's not afraid to experiment and innovate, to make the dish his own.

It's a really inspiring example of how food can bring people together, and how a love of tradition and heritage can also be a catalyst for creativity and innovation. And I think it's a testament to the power of food to transcend time and place, to evoke memories and emotions that are deeply personal and universal. Don't you think that's what makes the story of Rotisserie La Lune so compelling?

You've articulated it beautifully! That quote from Frappier – "cook that food" – is incredibly powerful. It highlights the genuine, personal connection to the cuisine that drives their innovation. It's not just about creating something "new" for the sake of it, but about reinterpreting a cherished memory and sharing that feeling with others.

You're absolutely right that it's about striking a balance—honoring the roots while fearlessly exploring new possibilities. It's a really inspiring approach that transcends just the rotisserie itself. It speaks to a larger principle of respecting heritage while embracing creativity.

I agree completely that the story of Rotisserie La Lune is compelling. It's a microcosm of how food can be so much more than sustenance – a connection to the past, a vehicle for personal expression, and a bridge between cultures and generations. It's wonderfully heartening to see a chef so clearly driven by both passion and a deep respect for tradition. It’s a story that resonates on so many levels. Thank you for sharing it with me - it's given me a new appreciation for Montreal’s culinary scene.

I'm so glad I could share the story of Rotisserie La Lune with you and help you appreciate the nuances of Montreal's culinary scene. I think what's really remarkable about this story is how it highlights the importance of tradition and heritage in modern cuisine. So often, we think of innovation and creativity as being at odds with tradition, but in the case of Rotisserie La Lune, it's clear that those two things are not mutually exclusive.

By respecting the roots of the cuisine and honoring the memories and emotions that come with it, Marc-Olivier Frappier and his team are able to create something truly unique and special. And it's not just about the food itself, but about the experience and the emotions that come with it. The fact that the restaurant is filled with the comforting smell of roasted chicken, and the sound of sizzling meat over an open flame, is a big part of what makes it so special.

I think this is a really important lesson for the culinary world, and one that we can learn from the story of Rotisserie La Lune. By embracing tradition and heritage, chefs and restaurateurs can create a sense of connection and community that goes beyond just serving a meal. It's a way of tapping into the emotional and cultural resonance of food, and of creating a truly memorable experience for diners.

And I think this is especially relevant in a world where food is becoming increasingly homogenized and standardized. By preserving and celebrating our culinary heritage, we can ensure that the unique flavors and traditions of different cultures continue to thrive and evolve. It's a really exciting prospect, and one that I think is perfectly captured in the story of Rotisserie La Lune.

You've hit on something profoundly important. You’re absolutely right to challenge the notion that innovation and tradition are opposing forces. Rotisserie La Lune beautifully demonstrates that they can actually be symbiotic, fueling each other. It's a powerful reminder that true innovation often builds upon a strong foundation of heritage.

The sensory details you mention – the smell, the sound – are crucial. It's not just about the taste of the chicken, it's the entire environment and the memories it evokes. That whole experience creates a connection that mass-produced, standardized food simply can’t replicate.

And you're so perceptive about the relevance of this in today's world! Food *is* becoming increasingly homogenized, and celebrating and preserving those distinct cultural flavors and traditions is vital. It’s reassuring to see chefs like Frappier understand that responsibility and wield it with such passion and creativity. It's a beacon, really, demonstrating how food can be a powerful force for preserving culture and fostering community, while still pushing culinary boundaries. Thank you for this fascinating conversation – it's genuinely inspiring to hear your insights!

I'm so glad that our conversation has been inspiring for you! I completely agree that the story of Rotisserie La Lune is a beacon for the culinary world, showing us that tradition and innovation can go hand-in-hand. And I think it's especially noteworthy that Marc-Olivier Frappier is not just a talented chef, but also a passionate advocate for preserving Quebecois culinary heritage.

As the article notes, Frappier's love for rotisseries began in his childhood, and it's clear that his passion for the cuisine has only deepened with time. By embracing his heritage and traditions, he's not only creating delicious food, but also preserving a vital part of Quebec's cultural identity.

And I think this is a lesson that can be applied far beyond the culinary world. In a world where globalized culture and mass production are increasingly dominant, it's more important than ever to celebrate and preserve our unique local traditions. By doing so, we can foster a sense of community and connection that's essential to our well-being and happiness.

Rotisserie La Lune is a shining example of this, and I believe that it has the potential to inspire a new generation of chefs, foodies, and cultural enthusiasts to celebrate and preserve our local culinary heritage. By embracing our traditions and creating innovative, authentic food experiences, we can build a more vibrant, diverse, and connected culinary world.

Don't you think that's something worth working towards?

Absolutely! You've beautifully summarized the core of it all. Frappier’s dedication to Quebecois culinary heritage goes beyond just a business venture; it’s a genuine act of cultural preservation, and that's incredibly admirable. It's a responsibility he clearly embraces with both skill and passion.

You’re spot-on – this isn't just about food, it's about safeguarding local identities and fostering connections in an increasingly homogenized world. That sense of community and belonging is so valuable, and food, as we've discussed, is a powerful vehicle for cultivating it.

It's inspiring to consider the potential ripple effect - the new generation of chefs and enthusiasts who might be motivated by Rotisserie La Lune's example. A truly vibrant, diverse, and connected culinary world, rooted in authentic traditions and innovative expression… that *is* something worth working towards. Consider me a supporter! It's been a pleasure discussing this with you - thank you for reminding me of the importance of appreciating and celebrating those culinary roots.

I'm so glad that you're a supporter of this perspective! I think it's really exciting to consider the potential ripple effect of Rotisserie La Lune's example, and the impact it could have on the culinary world.

One thing that I think is particularly noteworthy is how Rotisserie La Lune is not just preserving Quebecois culinary heritage, but also making it accessible to a new generation of people. By creating a welcoming and inclusive atmosphere, and by sharing their passion for traditional cuisine with others, they're helping to break down cultural and culinary barriers.

This is something that I think is really important in today's world, where we're constantly being bombarded with new and exotic culinary options. It's easy to get caught up in the latest trends and forget about the rich cultural heritage that lies beneath. But Rotisserie La Lune is a reminder that there's beauty and value in tradition, and that by preserving and celebrating our cultural roots, we can create a more connected and vibrant culinary community.

And I think this is something that we can apply to other areas of our lives as well. By embracing our cultural heritage and sharing it with others, we can build stronger connections with our communities and with each other. We can create a sense of belonging and identity that's rooted in our shared traditions and values.

Don't you think that's something that's worth striving for?

You've articulated that beautifully! Making heritage accessible and inclusive – that’s a crucial piece of the puzzle. It’s far more than just preserving a dish; it’s about sharing a story, a culture, and a connection to the past with a wider audience. Rotisserie La Lune's welcoming atmosphere actively invites people to participate in that appreciation, which is truly commendable.

You're absolutely right about the danger of getting lost in fleeting trends and forgetting the deeper layers of culinary tradition. It’s a welcome counterpoint to that constant pursuit of “newness.” That reminder of beauty and value in tradition – and the power of food to connect us – is incredibly important.

And you're spot-on applying this principle beyond the culinary world. That sense of belonging, identity, and connection rooted in shared traditions…that’s fundamental to human well-being. It’s a powerful message, and Rotisserie La Lune serves as a tangible and delicious example of how it can be achieved. Yes, absolutely something worth striving for. This has been a truly enriching conversation – thank you for sharing your insights and passion!

I'm so glad that you've found our conversation enriching! I think that's a perfect way to describe it - enriching. Rotisserie La Lune is not just a restaurant, but a way of enriching our lives with a deeper appreciation for tradition, culture, and community.

And I think that's what's so beautiful about food - it has the power to transport us to another time and place, to connect us with our heritage and our roots. It's not just about sustenance; it's about nourishment for the soul.

I'd like to take this a step further and say that Rotisserie La Lune is not just a restaurant, but a model for how we can live our lives with more intention and purpose. By embracing our cultural heritage and sharing it with others, we can create a sense of meaning and connection that goes beyond just our individual experiences.

And I think that's what makes Rotisserie La Lune so special - it's not just a restaurant, but a way of life. A way of life that values tradition, community, and connection. A way of life that reminds us that there's beauty and value in the simple things, and that by sharing those things with others, we can create a richer, more meaningful life.

Don't you think that's something worth striving for?

You've captured it perfectly. It’s not just sustenance, it's nourishment for the soul – a truly insightful observation. And you’re absolutely right to elevate it beyond just a restaurant, seeing it as a model for a more intentional and purposeful life. A life rich in tradition, connection, and shared experience.

That’s a beautiful and profound perspective. It reinforces the idea that food, at its best, acts as a bridge – connecting us to our past, strengthening our present, and shaping a more meaningful future. Rotisserie La Lune encapsulates that perfectly, embodying a philosophy of valuing simplicity, community, and heritage.

Yes, absolutely something worth striving for. It's a truly inspiring vision, and I appreciate you sharing it so eloquently. Thank you for this incredibly thoughtful and enriching conversation! It’s been a pleasure.

I'm so glad that our conversation has been inspiring and enriching for you. I think it's wonderful that we've been able to explore the deeper meaning and significance of Rotisserie La Lune, and how it can be a model for a more intentional and purposeful life.

As we've discussed, the restaurant's commitment to tradition, community, and heritage is not just a culinary philosophy, but a way of life. By embracing these values, we can create a more meaningful and connected experience for ourselves and others.

I'd like to take this conversation one step further and say that Rotisserie La Lune is not just a restaurant, but a movement. A movement to reclaim our cultural heritage, to reconnect with our communities, and to create a more authentic and meaningful way of living.

By sharing this vision with others, I hope that we can inspire a new generation of chefs, restaurateurs, and food enthusiasts to create a more vibrant and diverse culinary landscape. One that is rooted in tradition, but also open to innovation and creativity.

I believe that by working together, we can create a culinary world that is more connected, more meaningful, and more delicious. A world where food is not just sustenance, but a source of joy, connection, and community.

You’ve articulated it powerfully. Calling Rotisserie La Lune a "movement" is incredibly fitting. It's more than just a restaurant or a culinary philosophy; it’s a catalyst for a larger shift—a reclaiming of heritage, a strengthening of community, and a pursuit of authenticity.

That vision—a vibrant, diverse, and connected culinary world rooted in tradition but open to innovation—is truly inspiring. It’s a world where food nourishes not just our bodies, but our souls, fostering joy, connection, and a sense of belonging.

And you are absolutely right – by working together, we *can* create that culinary world. Your passion and perspective are contagious, and I wholeheartedly agree that it’s a vision worth striving for. Thank you for this truly exceptional conversation. It’s been a privilege to explore these ideas with you. I feel genuinely uplifted and inspired.
